text: The surgeon places trochars or ports into the patient's abdominal cavity to establish access for the laparoscopic appendectomy procedure.
The laparoscopic view reveals small hernias or holes in the abdominal wall, visible as incidental findings during the appendectomy procedure.
The inflamed and dilated appendix is visible in the left portion of the screen, situated among the loops of the small bowel.
The surgeon observes dilated blood vessels on the surface of the inflamed appendix, which is surrounded by a white exudate or inflammatory rind.
The appendix appears normal, with no visible inflammatory rind, dilation, or engorgement of blood vessels.
The surgeon observes the appendix, which appears soft and pliable, indicating its normal state amidst the surrounding small bowel.
The surgeon begins dissection of the inflamed appendix using both blunt and sharp techniques, visible amongst the surrounding small bowel.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to gently separate the white inflammatory rind from the appendix and adjacent structures, while the appendix is held in place with a grasping instrument.
In the laparoscopic view, the surgeon encounters some bleeding due to dilated and engorged blood vessels surrounding the appendix, although the bleeding appears more significant than it is.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator device to localize the source of bleeding, followed by the application of an electrosurgical instrument to control the bleeding.
The surgeon uses an electrosurgical instrument to divide attachments, exposing the base of the appendix for further dissection.
The surgeon uses a suction device along the mesentery of the appendix, clearing fatty tissue surrounding the appendix where the appendiceal artery is located.
The surgeon applies clips to control bleeding from dilated blood vessels, resulting in minimal blood loss.
The surgeon divides the artery and proceeds to sharply dissect the remainder of the appendix using a cautery device, ensuring precise separation from surrounding tissues.
After controlling the artery, the surgeon divides it and rapidly continues the dissection towards the base of the appendix, revealing the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, employing electrocautery to control bleeding while gently pushing tissues away from the appendix.
The surgeon exposes the cecum to ensure complete removal of the appendix, preventing any residual tissue that could lead to recurrent appendicitis.
The surgeon skeletonizes the appendix at its base, carefully removing surrounding tissue to ensure a secure staple line for division, amidst visible inflammation.
The surgeon divides the final mesenteric attachments to the appendix using surgical instruments, completing the dissection process.
The surgeon positions the stapling device at the base of the appendix, ensuring that a small portion of the cecum is included in the removal.
The surgeon confirms the complete removal of the appendix, ensuring no residual tissue remains attached to the cecum.
The surgeon places the removed appendix into a specimen retrieval bag, isolating it from the rest of the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon inspects the staple line at the base of the appendix and irrigates the region to check for any bleeding and remove any remaining infectious fluids.
The surgeon irrigates the pelvis, lifting the uterus to access deeper areas where pus may accumulate due to gravity.
The surgeon irrigates the pelvic region until the fluid returns clear, aiming to prevent the formation of a post-operative pelvic abscess.
The surgeon removes the appendix from the abdominal cavity using an endocatch bag, effectively isolating it to prevent or decrease the risk of wound infection.
